content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

March 31, 1983


Ira Bonde
113 Princeton Road
Menlo Park, CA 94025

RE: Our Advice No. A-83-077

Dear Mr. Bonde:

Thank you for your letter of March 20, 1983, concerning
a possible conflict between your position on the San Mateo
Mosquito Abatement District and a possible position on the
West Bay Sanitary District. The Political Reform Act,
which is administered by the Fair Political Practices
Commission, deals only with the question of possible
conflicts between an official's private financial interests
and his actions as a public official. Thus the fact that
you might hold two different public positions could create
no conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act.

If you are concerned about whether holding these two
positions would create a conflict under any other law, I
suggest that you contact your county attorney. If you have
any other questions about the Political Reform Act, please
feel free to write to us again.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
Chief
Legal Division

BAM:ms

sections.question: Whether holding a position on the San Mateo Mosquito Abatement District while also potentially holding a position on the West Bay Sanitary District would create a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion: The fact that you might hold two different public positions could create no conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act. The Political Reform Act deals only with the question of possible conflicts between an official's private financial interests and his actions as a public official.
sections.question_synthetic: Can an official simultaneously hold positions on both the San Mateo Mosquito Abatement District and the West Bay Sanitary District without violating conflict of interest provisions under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Holding two different public positions does not create a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act, which only addresses conflicts between private financial interests and official actions. If concerns exist regarding conflicts under other laws, the county attorney should be consulted.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter format with clear question and conclusion sections. Signed by Barbara A. Milman, Chief of Legal Division. Document dated March 31, 1983, responding to March 20, 1983 inquiry.

QUESTION: Whether holding a position on the San Mateo Mosquito Abatement District while also potentially holding a position on the West Bay Sanitary District would create a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: The fact that you might hold two different public positions could create no conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act. The Political Reform Act deals only with the question of possible conflicts between an official's private financial interests and his actions as a public official.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that holding two simultaneous public positions on the San Mateo Mosquito Abatement District and West Bay Sanitary District does not constitute a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act, as that statute addresses only conflicts between private financial interests and official duties.
